title: Patbingsu Red Bean Shaved Ice
description: Patbingsu is a beloved Korean dessert, a refreshing symphony of textures and flavors. Creamy, finely shaved milk ice serves as the base, generously crowned with sweet, tender adzuki red beans, chewy rice cakes, and a vibrant medley of fresh fruits. It's a perfect balance of cool sweetness and delightful chewiness, making it an iconic treat, especially cherished during warmer months.

ingredients.notes: adjust to taste, for red beans
optional, for extra sweetness in ice base
cut into bite-sized pieces (optional, but highly recommended)

instructions.title: Prepare the Adzuki Beans (Initial Boil)
instructions.text: Rinse the dried adzuki beans thoroughly under cold running water. In a medium pot, combine the rinsed beans with enough fresh water to cover them by about 2 inches. Bring the water to a rolling boil over high heat. Once boiling, immediately drain all the water from the pot. This crucial step helps to remove any bitterness from the beans and ensures a cleaner flavor.

instructions.title: Cook the Adzuki Beans until Tender
instructions.text: Return the blanched adzuki beans to the pot. Add 6-8 cups of fresh water, ensuring the beans are well submerged. Bring the water to a boil again, then reduce the heat to low, cover the pot, and simmer gently for 1 to 1.5 hours, or until the beans are very tender and easily mashable between your fingers. Check the water level periodically and add more hot water if needed to keep the beans covered.

instructions.title: Sweeten and Thicken the Red Bean Paste (Danpat)
instructions.text: Once the beans are tender, drain most of the cooking water, leaving just enough to barely cover the beans (about 1/4 to 1/2 cup). Add the granulated sugar (starting with 1/2 cup and adjusting later) and salt. Using a spoon or potato masher, lightly mash about a third of the beans against the side of the pot to create a creamy texture while leaving the rest whole for bite. Continue to simmer gently, stirring occasionally, for 10-15 minutes, until the mixture thickens to a jam-like consistency. Taste and add more sugar if desired. Let the sweet red beans cool completely before using.

instructions.title: Prepare the Milk Ice Base (Uyu Bingsu)
instructions.text: In a large bowl, whisk together the whole milk, 1/2 cup sweetened condensed milk, and the optional 2 tablespoons of granulated sugar until all ingredients are well combined and the sugar is dissolved. Pour this milk mixture into shallow metal trays, freezer-safe containers, or ice cube trays. Place them in the freezer for at least 6-8 hours, or preferably overnight, until the milk is completely solid and frozen through.

instructions.title: Prepare Toppings and Shave the Ice
instructions.text: While the milk ice is freezing, prepare your remaining toppings. If using mini tteok, boil them in water for 2-3 minutes until they become soft and chewy, then drain and rinse with cold water to prevent sticking. Slice the fresh strawberries and dice the kiwi. Cut injeolmi into small, bite-sized pieces if using. Once the milk ice is solid, remove it from the freezer. Using a dedicated bingsu machine or a sturdy food processor (pulse in short bursts), shave or finely process the frozen milk until it resembles soft, fluffy snow.

instructions.title: Assemble and Serve Your Patbingsu
instructions.text: Divide the finely shaved milk ice among individual serving bowls, mounding it high. Generously spoon the cooled sweet red beans (danpat) over the center of the shaved ice. Artfully arrange the sliced strawberries, diced kiwi, softened mini tteok, and injeolmi pieces around the red beans. Finish with a generous drizzle of additional sweetened condensed milk to taste. Serve immediately with long spoons, encouraging guests to mix the layers as they enjoy.

tips: For the best texture, use a dedicated bingsu machine which creates incredibly fine, snow-like ice. If using a food processor, pulse in short bursts to avoid over-processing and melting.
Use high-quality adzuki beans for the best flavor. Korean mini tteok (garae-tteok or songpyeon-tteok) are ideal for their chewy texture; look for them in Korean markets.
Patbingsu is best enjoyed immediately after assembly. Encourage guests to mix the layers as they eat to enjoy a harmonious blend of flavors and textures in every spoonful.
article:
## History of Patbingsu Red Bean Shaved Ice

Patbingsu's origins trace back to the Joseon Dynasty, where it began as a simple dessert made with shaved ice, red beans, and sometimes fruit. Initially, it was a delicacy enjoyed by royalty and the elite, with ice harvested from frozen rivers and stored in icehouses. Over centuries, as ice became more accessible, Patbingsu evolved into a popular street food. In the 1950s and 60s, with the introduction of mechanical ice shavers, it became widely available. The classic version with sweet red beans (pat) remains a timeless favorite, though modern variations with fruit, condensed milk, and various toppings have emerged. Today, Patbingsu is an iconic summer dessert, cherished across all generations in Korea for its refreshing qualities and delightful combination of flavors and textures, symbolizing cool relief and sweet indulgence.

## Why This Recipe Works

This recipe captures the authentic essence of Patbingsu by focusing on the perfect balance of key components. The homemade sweet red beans (danpat) are prepared to an ideal texture—tender yet slightly chunky—and sweetened just right, avoiding an overly sugary taste. The milk ice base, infused with condensed milk, shaves into a creamy, melt-in-your-mouth snow that provides a rich counterpoint to the beans. The inclusion of chewy mini tteok and fresh, vibrant fruits adds crucial textural contrast and bursts of natural sweetness and acidity, preventing the dessert from being monotonous. This combination ensures a refreshing, satisfying, and texturally dynamic experience that is genuinely Korean.

## Chef Tips for Perfection

Achieving the perfect Patbingsu hinges on a few crucial details. Firstly, the quality of your adzuki beans is paramount; fresh, plump beans will yield the best "danpat." Don't rush the simmering process; the beans must be truly tender. For the ice, ensure your milk mixture is completely frozen; partially frozen ice will result in slushy rather than snowy bingsu. If you don't have a bingsu machine, freezing the milk in thin layers in a metal pan and then scraping it with a sturdy spatula can mimic a finer texture than a food processor. Finally, assemble just before serving. The delicate shaved ice melts quickly, and immediate consumption ensures the optimal frosty texture and freshness of all toppings.

## Serving Suggestions

Patbingsu is traditionally a communal dessert, perfect for sharing on a hot day. Serve it in large, wide bowls that allow for easy mixing of the layers. Provide long-handled spoons for digging deep into the icy goodness. While the classic toppings of sweet red beans, tteok, and fruit are essential, feel free to offer additional Korean-inspired accompaniments like a scoop of green tea ice cream (nokcha ice cream), a sprinkle of roasted soybean powder (injeolmi garu), or a drizzle of misutgaru (roasted grain powder) syrup for extra nuttiness. A side of additional condensed milk or a small bowl of extra red beans allows guests to customize their sweetness and richness.

jsonLd.keywords: Korean, Korean, authentic, traditional, desserts, Korean, Patbingsu, Red Bean Shaved Ice, Dessert, Authentic, Traditional, Summer Treat, Adzuki Beans, Bingsu, Sweet Red Beans, Shaved Ice Milk
recipeIngredient: 1/2 to 3/4 cup Granulated sugar (adjust to taste, for red beans)
1/2 cup Sweetened condensed milk (for milk ice base)
2 tablespoons Granulated sugar (optional, for extra sweetness in ice base)
1 cup Mini tteok (rice cakes) (boiled until soft)
1/2 cup Injeolmi (roasted soybean powder rice cakes) (cut into bite-sized pieces (optional, but highly recommended))
for drizzling Sweetened condensed milk (to taste)
